quiz.title: Unit 7 Quiz 9, Grade 11 Biology
quiz.subtitle: Genetics: Mutations, Cancer, and DNA Repair
quiz.subject: Biology
quiz.topic: Mutation Types, Somatic vs Germline Mutations, Tumor Suppressor Genes, Oncogenes, Metastasis, Benign vs Malignant, DNA Repair

questions.question: What is a mutation?
options: A normal stage of the cell cycle
A change in the DNA sequence
questions.explanation: A mutation is any change in the nucleotide sequence of DNA. This can range from a single base change to large chromosomal alterations.

questions.question: Mutations can occur during:
questions.explanation: Mutations often occur during DNA replication when errors in base pairing are made. They can also occur due to environmental factors like radiation or chemicals damaging DNA.

questions.question: Select all that apply: This can cause mutation:
questions.explanation: Mutations can be caused by ultraviolet radiation (sunlight), various chemicals (mutagens), and errors during DNA replication. Saline solution (salt water) does not typically cause mutations.

questions.question: A mutation that occurs in body cells is called a:
questions.explanation: A somatic mutation occurs in body cells (non-reproductive cells) and is not passed to offspring. Germline mutations occur in gametes and can be inherited.

questions.question: Which mutations can be passed to offspring?
questions.explanation: Germline mutations occur in reproductive cells (sperm or egg) and can be passed to offspring. Somatic mutations occur in body cells and are not heritable.

questions.question: Cancer occurs when:
questions.explanation: Cancer results from uncontrolled cell division due to mutations in genes that regulate the cell cycle. Cells divide without normal controls, forming tumors.

questions.question: Which type of gene normally prevents uncontrolled cell division?
questions.explanation: Tumor suppressor genes normally regulate cell division and prevent uncontrolled growth. When mutated, they lose function, allowing cancer to develop (e.g., p53, Rb).

questions.question: Oncogenes:
questions.explanation: Oncogenes are mutated forms of proto-oncogenes that normally promote cell division. When mutated, they become overactive and promote excessive, uncontrolled cell division.

questions.question: Which process allows cancer cells to spread to other parts of the body?
questions.explanation: Metastasis is the process by which cancer cells break away from the primary tumor, travel through blood or lymph vessels, and establish secondary tumors in other parts of the body.

questions.question: A tumor that does not spread to other tissues is known as:
questions.explanation: A benign tumor remains localized and does not invade surrounding tissues or metastasize. Malignant tumors are cancerous and can spread to other parts of the body.

questions.question: Which type of mutation involves a change in a single nucleotide?
questions.explanation: A gene mutation (point mutation) involves a change in one or a few nucleotides within a gene. Chromosomal mutations affect larger segments of chromosomes.

questions.question: DNA repair mechanisms help:
options: Ensure cell division happens at moderate speed
questions.explanation: DNA repair mechanisms correct damage to DNA, preventing mutations from accumulating. They don't prevent mutations from happening but fix them after they occur.

questions.question: What is the most common effect of many mutations?
questions.explanation: Most mutations are neutral (silent) and have no noticeable effect on the organism. They occur in non-coding regions or do not alter protein function.

questions.question: A mutation that does not change the amino acid sequence of a protein is called a/an:
questions.explanation: A silent mutation is a change in DNA that does not alter the amino acid sequence due to the redundancy of the genetic code. The protein remains unchanged.

questions.question: Select all that apply. Which of the following is true about mutations?
questions.explanation: Mutations can be beneficial (providing an advantage), harmful (causing disease or reduced fitness), or neutral (no noticeable effect). They are not all harmful.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered mutation types and cancer biology! 🧬
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review the difference between oncogenes and tumor suppressor genes.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on somatic vs germline mutations and metastasis.
scoreMessages.0: Please review mutation causes, cancer development, and DNA repair mechanisms.
